title: Pass schematic port facing directions to the trace solver
description: Passes each schematic ports true facing direction to the trace solver to improve pin snapping accuracy and prevent misalignment of net labels.
body: ## What When building the trace-solver input, forward each schematic port's true facing direction as `_facingDirection` (mapped from `schematic_port.facing_direction` via the existing `convertFacingDirectionToElbowDirection`). Why The chip box we hand the solver comes from getSchematicComponentWithTextBounds, i.e. it's grown to enclose the component's reference-designator/value text. For a small part with a large ref designator (a resistor labeled R_FAULT_PULLUP, a decoupling cap, …) that box is much bigger than the part, so the component's ports sit inside the box. The solver has to move an inside-pin to a box edge, and with no facing information it guessed from geometry — picking the nearest edge, which for a horizontal resistor whose wide ref text widened the box is the bottom. Both terminals were treated as facing y-, so the connection/net labels dropped below the part instead of leaving from its sides. We already compute the correct facing from the schematic symbol (schematic_port.facing_direction, used elsewhere for matchpack and net-label insertion). We just weren't sending it to the solver, forcing it to re-derive (and mis-derive) it. This passes the ground truth instead of relying on the guess. The roomy text-inclusive box is unchanged — it still gives parts breathing room, and the existing extendTraceEndpointsToReachPinsInsideExpandedBoundingBox still reconnects the routed trace back to the true port. Only the snap direction for these mis-detected pins changes. Depends on Requires the trace-solver change that snaps _facingDirection-carrying pins to the edge along that direction instead of the nearest edge (and stops clearing it): @tscircuit/schematic-trace-solver PR https://github.com/tscircuit/schematic-trace-solver/pull/645. Bump the dependency to that release. Without it, the solver's correctPinsInsideChips overwrites the facing we send here and nothing changes.

title: Count all rendered elements in getComprehensivePcbBounds
description: Fixes rendering issues by ensuring that getComprehensivePcbBounds includes the extent of every element the renderer draws, preventing clipping and ensuring accurate representation of PCB elements.
body:
Fixes #603. Builds on the repros in #604.

`getComprehensivePcbBounds` now includes the extent of every element the renderer draws:

- **pcb_plated_hole** — dispatch per shape (circle/oval/pill, rect-pad and rotated rect-pad, polygon pad) to the outer pad extent
- **pcb_via** — `outer_diameter`
- **pcb_smtpad** `rotated_rect` / `rotated_pill` — expand to the rotated axis-aligned box via `ccwRotationDegrees`
- **pcb_copper_pour** `brep` — outer-ring vertices; and rect pours now honor their `rotation`
- **pcb_silkscreen_pill** — center + width/height

Before → after (viewport now contains the full copper/silkscreen instead of clipping it or collapsing to a point):

![before/after grid](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/technologyet31-create/circuit-to-svg/assets/bounds-elements/bounds-before-after-grid.png)

- 7 numeric repros flip from `test.failing` to `test`; each of the 5 element snapshots regenerates to the un-clipped render (diffs visible in this PR)
- 5 existing snapshots (rotated-pad / plated-hole / error scenes) regenerate because their viewport legitimately grows — regenerated on linux to match CI
- Full suite green (verified on linux), `tsc --noEmit` clean



Rotated rect copper pour (was clipped to the un-rotated box):

![copper pour rotation](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/technologyet31-create/circuit-to-svg/assets/bounds-elements/copper-pour-rotation-before-after.png)

title: Add failing repros for elements missing from getComprehensivePcbBounds
description: Adds failing tests for elements missing from getComprehensivePcbBounds, including brep copper pours, plated holes, rotated SMT pads, vias, and silkscreen pills, to ensure they are accounted for in the rendering process.
body:
Failing repros for #603.

`getComprehensivePcbBounds` ignores the extent of `pcb_via`, `pcb_plated_hole` (all shapes), `pcb_smtpad` rotated_rect/rotated_pill, brep `pcb_copper_pour`, and `pcb_silkscreen_pill` — all of which the renderer draws — so they get clipped by the exported viewport.

Numeric `test.failing` repros (exact bounds) + committed snapshots of the clipped renders. The fix (#605) flips them to `test` and regenerates the snapshots to the un-clipped renders.

title: Preserve silkscreen text case in Gerber output
description: Fixes lowercase silkscreen text being rendered as uppercase glyph geometry in Gerber output
body:
## Summary

Fixes lowercase silkscreen text being rendered as uppercase glyph geometry in Gerber output.

## Root cause

`renderVectorText` iterated `element.text.toUpperCase()`, so Circuit JSON text like `tscircuit` was emitted with `TSCIRCUIT` glyph strokes. That made Gerber output differ from the PCB viewer.

## Fix

Iterate `element.text` directly so the selected alphabet glyphs preserve source text casing.

## Repro

Adds a tscircuit.com-style fixture generated from:

```tsx
<board width="20mm" height="10mm">
  <silkscreentext text="tscircuit" />
</board>
```

The test checks that `F_SilkScreen` emits the lowercase glyph stroke count. Before the fix, Gerber emitted 77 stroke segments, matching uppercase `TSCIRCUIT`; after the fix it emits 67, matching lowercase `tscircuit`.

## Before / after snapshots

| Before fix (repro #119) | After fix (this PR) |
| --- | --- |
| ![Before fix snapshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/technologyet31-create/circuit-json-to-gerber/2e07489a94af99df9c5a23a2262cd4d58352fcce/tests/repros/repro10/__snapshots__/tscircuit-text-20x10.snap.svg) | ![After fix snapshot](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/technologyet31-create/circuit-json-to-gerber/5c6a1b9a25ca26b619dab255933d013c3cb98539/tests/repros/repro10/__snapshots__/tscircuit-text-20x10.snap.svg) |

## Validation

- `bun test tests/repros/repro10/tscircuit-text-20x10.test.ts`
- `bun test`

GitHub checks are green: `test`, `format-check`, `type-check`, and Vercel.

title: fix: resolve Vercel build regression by aligning runframe, props, and 3d-viewer versions
description: Fixes deployment regression caused by incompatible package versions in the production dependency set, ensuring the app builds successfully with aligned versions of tscircuitrunframe, tscircuitprops, and tscircuit3d-viewer.
body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the deployment regression introduced by the package update in #3849.

The bot PR updated `@tscircuit/runframe`, but the resulting dependency graph was left in a partially upgraded state. This follow-up aligns the related `@tscircuit/*` packages so the app builds with a compatible set of versions again.

## What changed

- bumped `@tscircuit/runframe` to the compatible version
- aligned `@tscircuit/props` with the updated runtime package set
- aligned `@tscircuit/3d-viewer` so transitive `@tscircuit/core` / `circuit-json` dependencies resolve consistently
- refreshed `bun.lock` to reflect the corrected dependency graph

## Why this matters

This is not just a package update. It fixes a real deployment failure from #3849 by correcting a version compatibility regression in the production dependency set.

## Verification

- `bun install --frozen-lockfile`
- `bun run build`

Fixes the failing update PR: #3849

title: Improve same-net via merging
description: Extends same-net via merging to collapse nearby safe vias via hops, requires explicit connectivity data, and throws on malformed viaroute invariants instead of falling back.
body:
## Summary

- extend same-net via merging to collapse nearby safe via hops, not only physically overlapping vias
- require explicit connectivity data and throw on malformed via/route invariants instead of falling back
- add bugreport71 and focused solver coverage for via reduction and bad input cases

## Root Cause

`SameNetViaMergerSolver` only merged vias whose pads directly overlapped. In bugreport71, short top-bottom-top hops left nearby same-net vias in the final output even when a single via or later path simplification could remove the hop. The solver also tolerated missing connectivity or inconsistent via route data, which could hide bad solver state.

## Validation

```bash
BUN_UPDATE_SNAPSHOTS=1 bun test tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-direct-overlap-star.test.ts tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-unmapped-route-throws.test.ts tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-missing-transition-throws.test.ts tests/bugs/bugreport71-dd7d15.test.ts tests/bugs/simplification-trace-overlap-repro.test.ts --timeout 9999999
bun test tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-direct-overlap-star.test.ts tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-unmapped-route-throws.test.ts tests/solvers/same-net-via-merger-missing-transition-throws.test.ts tests/bugs/bugreport71-dd7d15.test.ts tests/bugs/simplification-trace-overlap-repro.test.ts --timeout 9999999
bun run build
```


```
  [AutoroutingPipelineSolver]
          |
          v
  [TraceSimplificationSolver]
          |
          | passes hdRoutes + obstacles + connMap
          v
  [UselessViaRemovalSolver]
          |
          | loops through every HighDensityRoute
          | creates one subsolver per route
          v
  [SingleRouteUselessViaRemovalSolver]
          |
          | breaks route into same-layer sections
          v
  [canEndpointConnectOnLayer]
          |
          | strict endpoint proof
          | uses exact route/root ids only
          v
  [canSectionMoveToLayer]
          |
          | checks whether a section can move layers
          | same-net route/obstacle is not a blocker
          v
  [optimized route with fewer useless vias]
          |
          v
  [SameNetViaMergerSolver]
          |
          | looks for nearby/overlapping vias on same net
          | uses canMoveViaTo before merging
          v
  [merged via output]
          |
          v
  [Path Simplification]
  ```

title: Fix stitch endpoint snapping to prevent closed-loop two-port traces
description: Fixes a route-stitching bug where a normal two-port connection could accidentally be stitched from one port back to the same port.
body: ## Summary Fixes a route-stitching bug where a normal two-port connection could accidentally be stitched from one port back to the same port. This caused closed-loop traces like: port A -> route geometry -> port B -> port A Later stages treated the trace as starting and ending at `port A`, so DRC reported `port B` as missing a connection. ## Root Cause `snapIslandEndpointToNearestTerminal` sorted the terminal list to choose the initial closest terminal, but then continued checking `params.terminals.slice(1)` from the original unsorted list. In `dataset01` circuit107, this skipped the real second terminal and snapped both route endpoints to `pcb_port_27`. ## Fix Use the same sorted terminal list for the full nearest-terminal search. This keeps the two endpoints separate, so the stitcher outputs an open route between the real ports instead of a closed loop.

title: Respect provided pin facingDirection when correcting pins inside expanded chip boxes
description: Fixes incorrect pin placement for components with long reference designators by ensuring pins are snapped to the correct edge based on their declared facing direction.
body: ## What `correctPinsInsideChips` moves any pin that sits *inside* its chip's bounding box out onto the nearest edge, so the router has a perimeter point to start from. This changes that step to honor an explicit `_facingDirection`: when a pin already declares which way it faces, snap it to the edge **along that direction** and keep the facing, instead of snapping to the geometrically nearest edge and clearing it. Nearest-edge snapping is still the fallback for pins with no `_facingDirection`, so existing behavior is unchanged. ## Why Consumers (e.g. `@tscircuit/core`) hand the solver a **text-inclusive** chip box — the component's box grown to enclose its reference-designator/value text. For a *small-variant* part with a *large* ref designator (a resistor labeled `R_FAULT_PULLUP`, a decoupling cap, …), that box is much wider/taller than the part, so the component's two ports end up **inside** the box rather than on an edge. `correctPinsInsideChips` then moved each port to the *nearest* edge. For a horizontal resistor whose wide ref text pushed the left/right edges far away, the nearest edge is the **bottom** — so both terminals were snapped downward and re-detected as facing `y-`. The result: connections/net labels dropped *below* the part instead of leaving from its left/right sides. The port's true facing is already known from the schematic symbol, and the solver already respects `pin._facingDirection` everywhere else (`pin._facingDirection ?? getPinDirection(...)`). The one place that ignored it — and actively cleared it — was this correction step. This makes it consistent: trust the declared facing. This is the solver half of the fix; the consumer supplies `_facingDirection` from the symbol (`@tscircuit/core` PR: https://github.com/tscircuit/core/pull/2614). Neither half works alone — sending the facing has no effect while this step overwrites it. ## Before / after - Before: small resistor with a long ref designator → both pins face `y-`, trace/net labels route below the part. - After: pins keep `x-`/`x+`, connections leave from the sides. Before: After:

Fix for the repros in #606. Adds the missing cases to `getComprehensivePcbBounds` so all three are included in the exported SVG viewport, and flips the repro assertions/snapshots to the fixed (un-clipped) state.

title: Render pcb_silkscreen_graphic to Gerber as a filled region
description: Renders the pcb_silkscreen_graphic as a filled region in the Gerber output, addressing a previous omission from the silkscreen layer.
body:
Follow-up to #121 (the "Not included" item from that PR). `pcb_silkscreen_graphic` — a **filled brep** (an outer ring plus inner rings that act as holes) — was also silently dropped from the Gerber silkscreen layer.

> ⚠️ **Stacked on #121.** This PR includes #121's commit until it merges; I'll rebase onto main afterward so only the graphic commit remains.

### Fix
Render the brep as a Gerber region, mirroring the existing `pcb_copper_pour` brep handling:
- **Outer ring** → a dark-polarity filled region (`G36`/`G37`).
- **Inner rings** → cut out with **clear polarity** (`%LPC*%`), then dark polarity is restored (`%LPD*%`) so following silkscreen isn't affected.

The type is newer than this repo's pinned circuit-json (0.0.426; the type exists in 0.0.443+), so it's matched structurally.

### Before / after
Rendered from the actual Gerber output (via `gerber-to-svg`) — a filled star with a cut-out hole:

![silkscreen graphic before/after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/technologyet31-create/circuit-json-to-gerber/assets/silkscreen/gerber-graphic-before-after.png)

### Tests
`generate-gerber-with-silkscreen-graphic.test.ts` — asserts the outer ring emits a region, inner rings are cut with clear polarity + dark restored, and it renders on the bottom layer. (Fails on `main`, passes here.) Also verified a normal silkscreen line drawn after a holed graphic still renders (no polarity leak). `tsc` + full suite green.

body:
Expands the `silkscreengraphic` documentation with a complete QR code workflow, demonstrating how to embed scannable QR codes into PCB silkscreens using inline SVG graphics.

This PR includes:

* New end-to-end QR code example using `<silkscreengraphic />`
* Example showing SVG-to-data-URL conversion for embedded graphics
* Complete PCB preview demonstrating QR code placement on the top silkscreen
* Best practices for QR code generation, quiet zones, and single-color artwork
* Guidance on recommended physical sizing for reliable scanning
* Recommendations for validating QR codes using PCB previews before manufacturing

This makes it straightforward for users to add production-ready QR codes to PCB silkscreens while following recommended practices for reliable fabrication and scanning.

title: ci: skip version bump PR checks
description: Skip regular PR CI jobs and benchmark instructions for branches starting with version-bumps, and add a Vercel ignore command for these branches.
body:
## Summary
- skip regular PR CI jobs when the PR head branch starts with `version-bumps/`
- skip the benchmark instructions comment for automated version bump PRs
- add a Vercel ignore command so external preview deployments are ignored for `version-bumps/**` branches

## Logic / fallacy review
- The skip predicate is branch-scoped, not path-scoped. This avoids the false equivalence that every `package.json` change is an automated version bump.
- The GitHub Actions guards keep `push` and manual/non-PR behavior unchanged by checking `github.event_name` before evaluating PR branch context.
- The `pull_request_target` workflow uses `github.event.pull_request.head.ref` because `github.head_ref` is not the reliable context for that event.
- The Vercel rule follows Vercel's documented ignore-command semantics: exit `0` ignores the deployment and exit `1` continues it.
- Residual risk: if a human intentionally uses a `version-bumps/` branch for non-version work, these checks will skip. That matches the repository's existing automated branch convention.

## Validation
- parsed changed workflow YAML with the repo-available `yaml` package
- parsed `vercel.json`
- ran `git diff --check`

title: Fix schematic routing regressions from pin-band penalty and chip-boundary reject
description: Fixes routing regressions caused by overly aggressive heuristics in schematic trace routing, ensuring valid routes are prioritized over longer detours and improving handling of chip boundary overlaps.
body: Two routing heuristics were over-aggressive and regressed several circuits once they were adopted downstream: - SchematicTraceSingleLineSolver2 ranked reroute candidates by `length + pinBandPenalty`, where the flat +10-per-in-band-segment penalty dwarfs schematic-scale lengths, so a longer "outside" detour beat a shorter valid route. Sort by length first and use the pin-band penalty only as a tiebreaker, so the outside preference never discards a shorter valid route. Fixes repro-missing-trace-netlabel and repro-rectifier-trace-overlap. - Example28Solver reroute hard-rejected any candidate running along a chip boundary, which discarded the best available route when every alternative overlapped a label or another trace. Replace the hard reject with a soft `chipBoundaryOverlap` score factor, ranked with a clean-route distinction: a route with zero label overlaps always wins (even if it hugs a boundary), but once a label overlap is unavoidable, prefer the route that doesn't also hug a boundary. Fixes repro-netlabel-overlap-trace while keeping bug-report-20260707T141421Z green. Neutral net-label-connector snapshot updates: example03, example32, example42, bug-report-20260706T220324Z.

title: Fix short schematic trace routing
description: Fixes routing issues for very short schematic connections between nearby pins, preventing unnecessary elbow overshoot and ensuring proper trace connections.
body: Very short schematic connections between nearby pins could route with unnecessary elbow overshoot, or fail entirely when one endpoint sat slightly inside a neighboring component's obstacle — causing a fallback net label to appear instead of a short trace. Change Add a short-distance path case in SchematicTraceSingleLineSolver2, checked before elbow routing (gated by MAX_SHORT_TRACE_DISTANCE). Normal-distance traces are unaffected — they still go through the existing elbow/collision search path. BEFORE: AFTER:

title: Add public accessors for KiCad PCB dimension fields

## Summary
- add public getters for parsed `Dimension` children: `layer`, `pts`, `height`, `style`, and `grText`
- add public getters for parsed `DimensionStyle` children: `thickness`, `arrowLength`, `textPositionMode`, `arrowDirection`, `extensionHeight`, `textFrame`, `extensionOffset`, and `keepTextAligned`

### Why We Need This

- kicad-to-circuit-json needs to convert KiCad board dimension objects into pcb_note_dimension. The parser already supports dimension, but only tstamp and uuid were publicly accessible. Other useful parsed fields like pts, layer, height, gr_text, and style.arrow_length were stored privately.

title: fix: properly subtract edge cutouts from board outline to prevent overlapping
description: Fixes overlapping issues by properly subtracting edge cutouts from the board outline using polygon-clipping library, ensuring a continuous path for Edge.Cuts.
body: fixes: #363 ### Change **Before:** Edge cutouts were blindly drawn as standalone `gr_circle` or `gr_poly` elements on `Edge.Cuts` directly on top of the board's rectangular outline, creating illegal intersecting loops. **After:** We introduced the `polygon-clipping` library to mathematically execute a boolean subtraction of all cutout shapes from the main board outline. The exporter now traces a single, continuous, non- intersecting path of `gr_line`segments for the entire board, naturally wrapping around edge-mounted cutouts.

title: Fix Git-installed package resolution for @tscircuit/monaco-code-editor and restore Vite consumer imports
description: Fixes a packaging bug in tscircuitmonaco-code-editor where Git installs did not produce a usable library package, aligning package entrypoints and automating builds for consumers.
body:
## Summary

Fixes a real consumer-facing packaging bug in `@tscircuit/monaco-code-editor` where Git installs did not produce a usable library package.

Before this change:
- `package.json` pointed consumers at `dist/index.js` and `dist/index.d.ts`
- Git installs did not include `dist/`
- downstream apps had to alias `@tscircuit/monaco-code-editor` to `src/index.ts`
- fresh Vite consumers could fail to resolve the package cleanly

## What changed

- aligned package entrypoints so runtime and type exports both resolve from `dist`
- added `prepare` so Git installs build the library automatically
- added a dedicated `tsconfig.lib.json` so package builds only target the library surface
- simplified the build script while keeping `dist/index.js` and `dist/index.d.ts` as the canonical outputs
- pinned the local compatibility dependencies needed for Vite/Cosmos example consumption

## User impact

This removes the need for downstream alias hacks and makes the package behave more like a normal consumable component library when installed from Git or from a registry.

Consumers should now be able to:

```ts
import { WorkspaceCodeEditor, useWorkspaceFiles } from "@tscircuit/monaco-code-editor"
```

without custom Vite aliases or ad-hoc type shims.

## Root cause

The package exported `dist` artifacts that were never present in Git installs, so consumers resolved entrypoints that did not exist. The library build also needed to be isolated from app/example files to keep `prepare` focused on generating the published package surface.

## Validation

- `bun run build`

title: Lay out decoupling cap groups as rail rows (Adds new DecouplingCapRowSolver)
description: Fixes the RP2040 decoupling capacitors stacking into one tall column instead of clean rail rows
body: Fixes the RP2040 decoupling capacitors stacking into one tall column instead of clean rail rows ( before: all 8 caps in a vertical column, rails zig-zagging down the left after: two rail rows, one per voltage ## Why it broke `@tscircuit/core` shipped a smaller capacitor symbol. Nothing else changed — nets, strong connections and gap settings in the `InputProblem` are byte-identical. Only the cap geometry moved: | | old | new (smaller symbol) | | -------- | ---------------- | -------------------- | | C12 size | `1.08 x 1.10` | `1.165 x 0.76` | | C12 pins | `(0.275, ±0.55)` | `(0, ±0.3)` | The leads got shorter, so the body went from **portrait to landscape**. The plates stayed wide. That mattered because **matchpack never decided how to arrange a decoupling cap group.** It identified the group, isolated it into its own partition, and then handed it to generic `calculate-packing`. The packer places each component at the outline point that minimizes the distance between same-network pads — and since every cap in a group shares the same power and ground net and they're all identical, that lines them up **along the shorter side of their body**. So the row/column choice was a function of the *body's aspect ratio*. The old symbol was taller than wide, so the packer produced a row. Right answer, wrong reason. The new symbol is wider than tall, so the same rule produced a column. The row was never implemented. It was a coincidence, and it was always fragile — even the old geometry was one character of value label away from breaking, because `chip.size.x` includes the rendered text (`100nF` → 1.165, `22nF` → 1.045, 0.12 per character): ``` OLD portrait caps, sweeping the label width: 4 chars size.x=0.96 -> ROW 5 chars size.x=1.08 -> ROW <- "100nF", what we shipped 6 chars size.x=1.20 -> SCATTER <- "1000pF" is a real value 7 chars size.x=1.32 -> COLUMN ``` (The label is not the trigger, though — strip the text entirely and the bare symbol is `0.9 x 0.6`, still landscape, still a column. A vertical cap has wide plates and short leads.) ## What actually decides the arrangement The **pin axis**, not the body. Every cap in a group bridges the same power net and the same ground net, so the two rails are only straight when the caps sit shoulder to shoulder *across* their pin axis. `IdentifyDecouplingCapsSolver` already guarantees that axis is vertical (it only accepts caps whose pins sit on opposite y sides), so the group is always a horizontal row. ## What this PR does **`DecouplingCapRowSolver`** — a new inner-partition solver, dispatched on `partitionType === "decoupling_caps"` via the extension point `PackInnerPartitionsSolver` already documents ("*new partition solvers are added by inserting a strategy before it*"). Sibling to `ParallelAlignedPassiveSolver`, which does the same thing for same-side passive groups. There is no packing problem to solve here — N caps on one line — so the axis comes from the pins and the row is placed directly, rather than coaxed out of a general-purpose packer. Caps keep their fixed rotation and their `chipMap` order, are pitched by `chip.size + decouplingCapsGap` (so value labels still earn their horizontal room), and are aligned so every positive pin lands on one rail. `SingleInnerPartitionPackingSolver` is **untouched** — zero-line diff. **`IdentifyDecouplingCapsSolver.findMainChipIdForCap`** — split into two resolvers. C9 (a bulk cap on the V1_1 rail) was being orphaned into its own single-chip partition, because the netlist writes the same electrical connection two ways: ```tsx U3.DVDD1: ["C18.1", "net.V1_1"] // names the pin -> strong pin-to-pin edge C9: { pin1: "net.V1_1", ... } // names the net -> no pin-to-pin edge ``` C9.1, C18.1 and U3.DVDD1 are all on V1_1 — C9 decouples U3 exactly like C18 does. But the main chip was resolved **only** from `pinStrongConnMap`, so a cap's grouping depended on how its connection happened to be typed. What actually makes a cap belong to a chip is that it bridges that chip's power rail and ground; a pin-to-pin edge is just one syntactic way to say so. So the resolver now falls back to the rail: the chip whose power pins sit on the cap's positive net. Ground is never consulted (every chip touches it) and 2-pin passives can't anchor a group (a cap doesn't decouple another cap).

title: Introduce Ratsnest-Free Schematic Solver Visualizations for Cleaner Bug Report Snapshots
description: Adds a hideRatsNet option to SchematicTracePipelineSolver so schematic debug visualizations can suppress unrouted ratsnest connection lines while preserving the existing default behavior.
body:
## Summary

Adds a `hideRatsNet` option to `SchematicTracePipelineSolver` so schematic debug visualizations can suppress unrouted ratsnest connection lines while preserving the existing default behavior.

## Changes

* Added `hideRatsNet` support to `SchematicTracePipelineSolverParams`.
* Preserved backward compatibility by allowing the constructor to accept either an `InputProblem` directly or a params object.
* Stores `hideRatsNet` on the solver and cloned input problem.
* Updated `visualizeInputProblem` to skip direct/net connection ratsnest lines when enabled.
* Ensures constructor params include `hideRatsNet` for solver replay/debug flows.
* Updated bug-report snapshot tests to use cleaner ratsnest-free visualizations.

## Impact

This makes schematic trace bug report snapshots easier to inspect by hiding noisy ratsnest overlays without changing routing behavior or solver output.

title: Snap power/ground net labels to a pin-aligned corner
description: Fixes the placement of powerground rail net labels to ensure they align with pins rather than floating at arbitrary points along traces.
body: Power/ground rail net labels (`VCC`, `GND`, `V3_3`, …) were placed at arbitrary points along their trace — typically at a mid-trace bend — instead of sitting as a clean stub aligned to a pin. In repro46, `V3_3` floated at a notch in the wire between C2 and U1, and `GND` looped awkwardly below, neither aligned to a pin. Two causes in `VccNetLabelCornerPlacementSolver` (the solver that snaps rail abels to trace corners): 1. It only processed labels where `netId === "VCC"`, so `GND`, `V3_3`, and every other named rail were never considered. 2. For the labels it did process, it snapped to the *closest* corner — which for a two-pin rail net is usually the mid-trace bend, not a pin-aligned position. ## Fix 1. **Process all vertical rail labels.** Detect rails by their fixed vertical orientation (`label.orientation === "y+" || "y-"`) rather than a `netId` string match. Rails point up/down; signal labels (`x+`/`x-`) point sideways and are left alone. 2. **Only snap to pin-aligned corners.** A corner is a candidate only when it lines up with one of the trace's pins along the label's stub axis (same x for a vertical label), so the label reads as a clean stub straight off that pin. Non-aligned corners aren't offered, which also leaves already-well-placed labels untouched. Motivation:

title: Give each pushed trace its own corridor when escaping a shared net-label block
description: Fixes overlapping trace corridors for different-net traces escaping a shared net-label block, ensuring they are routed in parallel without collision.
body:
Fixes #597.

When two different-net traces both collide with the **same** net-label block, `generateHorizontalSegmentPushCandidate` pushed each colliding vertical segment to `bounds.maxX + LABEL_CLEARANCE` — the *identical* corridor — so the two traces ended up drawn stacked on one line (a 2.6-unit collinear overlap right of `J2` at x≈15.7 in bug-report-20260706T213649Z).

### Fix
After computing the push X, slide the corridor outward past any **different-net** trace already routed there, so each escaping trace gets its own parallel corridor. Scoped to different nets only (same-net stacking is intentional / handled elsewhere), and bounded to `MAX_CORRIDOR_SHIFTS`.

### Before / after (traces only, J2 corridor)
![corridor before/after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/abdalraof-albarbar/schematic-trace-solver/assets/reroute-corridor/reroute-corridor-before-after.png)

### Verification
- Different-net trace overlaps in bug-report-20260706T213649Z drop from **4 → 2** — both vertical-corridor overlaps (x≈15.7 and x≈-10.65) are resolved. (The remaining 2 are unrelated *horizontal* overlaps near x≈17.7, a separate defect out of scope here.)
- Only that one bug-report snapshot changes; the rest of the suite is untouched (0 regressions across 106 tests). Regenerated the snapshot.

## Summary

Fixes browser 3D PNG downloads when `circuit-json-to-3d-png` is loaded through the CDN.

## Problem

The package dynamically imports `circuit-json-to-gltf`. In browser environments, the CDN GLTF import can fail, even though the application has already loaded and registered the module through `@tscircuit/internal-dynamic-import`.

## Fix

- Fall back to `globalThis.tscircuitDynamicModules["circuit-json-to-gltf"]`.
- Preserve module-load promise caching.
- Include the original import error as the cause when both loading paths fail.

This follows the established implementation in [circuit-json-to-step/lib/get-circuit-json-to-gltf-module.ts](https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-json-to-step/blob/main/lib/get-circuit-json-to-gltf-module.ts).

## Validation

- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun test` — 7 tests passed
- `bun run build`

This restores browser-compatible 3D PNG rendering without bundling the GLTF converter into the PNG package.

title: Sync Bun lockfile after merge
description: Updates bun.lock to match the current package.json after the merge to restore deterministic installs in CI and prevent frozen-lockfile failures.
body:
## What changed
This updates `bun.lock` to match the current `package.json` after the merge.

## Why it changed
CI was failing on `bun install --frozen-lockfile` because Bun detected lockfile drift and refused to rewrite the frozen lockfile.

## Root cause
`package.json` no longer includes `tsup`, but `bun.lock` still contained the old `tsup` dependency tree from before the merge.

## Impact

title: Fix monaco-code-editor package exports for component-library consumption in tscircuit.com
description: Fixes how tscircuitmonaco-code-editor is published to behave like a component library by switching package entrypoints from dist to srcindex.ts and ensuring styles are available for consumers.
body:
This PR fixes how `@tscircuit/monaco-code-editor` is published so it behaves like a component library instead of exporting a built `dist` bundle.

## What changed

- switch package entrypoints from `dist/*` to `src/index.ts`
- publish `src` instead of `dist`
- export `./styles.css` explicitly for consumers
- import library styles from the package entry so consumers get the expected UI
- remove the `tsup`/`dist` build path and keep library typechecking as the build validation step

## Why this changed

Downstream apps such as `tscircuit.com` already consume this package like a source-exported component library. The previous `dist`-based package shape was out of step with that usage and with the pattern already used in `@tscircuit/order-dialog`.

This change makes package consumption more predictable, ensures styles are available through the package surface, and aligns the published structure with the actual integration pattern.

## Impact

- fixes package-consumption issues for downstream apps like `tscircuit.com`
- aligns `@tscircuit/monaco-code-editor` with the component-library export pattern used elsewhere in the org
- removes an unnecessary bundle artifact from the publish flow

## Validation

- `bun run build`

title: Fix 3D package previews with native PNG rendering
description: Fixes 3D package preview generation by rendering circuit JSON directly to PNG instead of producing SVG and rasterizing it afterward.
body:
## Summary

Fixes 3D package preview generation by rendering circuit JSON directly to PNG instead of producing SVG and rasterizing it afterward.

- Replaces the legacy `circuit-json-to-simple-3d` path with `circuit-json-to-3d-png`
- Makes 3D package previews PNG-only and rejects unsupported `3d.svg` requests
- Preserves SVG and PNG output for schematic, PCB, and assembly previews
- Supports optional preview-width suffixes for native 3D PNG renders
- Updates `@tscircuit/internal-dynamic-import` to load `circuit-json-to-3d-png`
- Removes the unused direct `@tscircuit/simple-3d-svg` dependency

## Root cause

The package-image route used the legacy simple-3D SVG renderer, then rasterized its SVG output. This diverged from the native 3D PNG renderer already used by downloads.

## Impact

Package cards and CAD preview images now use a direct PNG rendering path, avoiding SVG conversion overhead and unsupported 3D SVG output.

## Validation

- `bunx tsc --noEmit`

PCB text export was shrinking requested `font_size` values by `1/1.5` and omitting explicit text thickness, which caused generated KiCad silkscreen and fab text to land below common fabrication minimums.

This change:
- preserves the source `font_size` for exported PCB text
- writes an explicit `(thickness 0.15)` for PCB text effects
- applies the fix consistently to:
- standalone silkscreen `gr_text`
- footprint silkscreen `fp_text`
- fabrication note `gr_text`
- centralizes PCB text effect creation in a shared helper

title: Remove manifold-3d dependency
description: Removes the stale direct manifold-3d dependency from the published tscircuit package and adds a regression test to ensure it is not included in future releases.
body:
## Summary
- remove the stale direct `manifold-3d` dependency from the published `tscircuit` package
- remove it from the root lockfile dependency list
- add a release regression test covering direct, optional, peer, bundled, and transitive production dependencies

## Root cause

The release sync in `scripts/copy-core-versions.ts` updates versions shared with `@tscircuit/core`, but it does not remove dependencies that core no longer declares. As a result, `tscircuit@0.0.2056` still directly installed `manifold-3d@3.5.1` even though the new core/copper-pour path uses `@tscircuit/manifold-2d`.

The regression test packs the local package, installs the resulting tarball in a clean temporary project, then verifies the generated npm lockfile and installed tree contain no `manifold-3d`. This exercises the actual published production dependency graph rather than the development workspace's peer-dependency lock artifacts.

## Verification
- `bun run build`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun test` — 3 passed
- clean packed-tarball install contains no `manifold-3d` manifest, lock entry, or installed package
- `bun install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts`
- Biome check and `git diff --check`

## Separate runtime finding

The copied runframe browser standalone still contains a runtime CDN reference to `manifold-3d@3.2.1` in its CAD viewer. That is not an npm dependency and is outside this focused package-tree fix; it should be migrated upstream in runframe separately.

title: Cache local autorouting results per phase
description: Caches local autorouting results for each phase to avoid redundant computations, improving efficiency in the autorouting process.
body:
## Summary

- cache each built-in local autorouting phase through `platform.localCacheEngine`
- namespace cache keys as `routes:core@<core version>:srj:<phase SRJ hash>`
- hash serialized phase SRJ with the same dependency-free, two-round FNV-1a approach already used by core's subcircuit cache
- restore cached phase traces before creating or starting `TscircuitAutorouter`
- write completed phase Simple Route JSON back to the cache
- bypass local caching entirely for custom `algorithmFn` autorouters
- treat cache read, parse, and write failures as non-fatal cache misses

## Why

Phased autorouting currently recomputes every built-in local routing phase even when the exact phase input has already been solved. Keying the cache from each phase's Simple Route JSON lets unchanged phases be reused independently, while the core-version namespace invalidates results whenever core is upgraded.

Custom autorouter functions are intentionally excluded for now because core cannot safely infer their implementation version or caching semantics.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/features/local-autorouting-phase-cache.test.tsx tests/components/normal-components/parts-engine-cache.test.tsx`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`

The regression test renders the same two-phase board twice with the built-in local autorouter, verifies two distinct `routes:`-prefixed versioned cache keys, and confirms the second render produces identical PCB traces without starting autorouting. It also verifies that a custom `algorithmFn` runs normally without any local cache reads or writes.

title: Warn when chips use reserved refdes prefixes
description: Emit a warning when generic chips use reference designator prefixes reserved for other component types, ensuring better adherence to naming conventions.
body:
## Summary

- emit `source_refdes_convention_warning` when a generic chip uses a refdes prefix reserved for connectors, transistors, capacitors, resistors, inductors, crystals, fuses, switches, or test points
- keep actual connectors and jumpers exempt from the `J*` chip warning

## Why

Generic `<chip>` components source-render as `simple_chip`, but do not expose a default source ftype through their component config. As a result, the existing convention checker skipped them entirely and could not flag refdes names that strongly indicate a more specific component primitive should be used.

## Impact

Authors now receive a Circuit JSON warning for generic chips named with `J*`, `Q*`, `C*`, `R*`, `L*`, `Y*`, `X*`, `F*`, `S*`, or `TP*`. Valid `U*` chips and actual connector/jumper components are unchanged.

title: Fix 3D PNG downloads by preloading the GLTF converter
description: Fixes the 3D PNG download flow by preloading circuit-json-to-gltf before loading circuit-json-to-3d-png
body:
## Summary

Fixes the 3D PNG download flow by preloading `circuit-json-to-gltf` before loading `circuit-json-to-3d-png`.

## Root cause

The 3D PNG exporter dynamically depends on the GLTF converter. The application previously loaded only the PNG exporter, leaving its GLTF dependency unavailable during exporter initialization. This caused 3D PNG downloads to fail at runtime.

## Change

Load and cache `circuit-json-to-gltf` through the existing shared dynamic-module promise map before loading `circuit-json-to-3d-png`.

This follows the established initialization pattern used by converters that dynamically import the GLTF converter and does not duplicate module loads.

## User impact

Restores the browser 3D PNG download flow.

## Validation

- `bun run typecheck`
- Confirmed 3D PNG download works end-to-end in the application.

## Supporting dependency hardening

These related changes improved the underlying module delivery path, but this PR is the application-level fix that coordinates the 3D PNG exporter and GLTF converter:

- [jscdn.tscircuit.com #11](https://github.com/tscircuit/jscdn.tscircuit.com/pull/11)
- [PoppyGL #32](https://github.com/tscircuit/poppygl/pull/32)
- [circuit-json-to-step #119](https://github.com/tscircuit/circuit-json-to-step/pull/119)

title: Reuse current Circuit JSON builds where the filesystem hash hasn't changed
description: Adds a deterministic MD5 hash for the project source filesystem to generated source_project_metadata, allowing commands to reuse existing Circuit JSON build artifacts when the source filesystem hash matches, thus avoiding redundant evaluations and improving efficiency.
body:
## Summary

- add a deterministic MD5 hash for the project source filesystem to generated `source_project_metadata`
- reuse the normal `dist/.../circuit.json` build artifact when its stored hash matches the current source filesystem
- route export, check, snapshot, and simulate generation through the cache-aware helper
- keep cache misses read-only so partial command-specific generation cannot replace the full build artifact
- keep explicit `tsci build` on the direct generation path so it always rebuilds

## Why

Commands such as export currently regenerate Circuit JSON even when `tsci build` already produced an artifact for unchanged source code. Comparing `source_filesystem_md5_hash` lets these commands safely reuse the existing build and avoid redundant evaluation, parts lookup, placement, and routing work.

Generated Circuit JSON files and build/cache directories are excluded from the source hash so running exports does not invalidate an otherwise current build.

## Dependency

This uses the `source_filesystem_md5_hash` metadata field introduced by tscircuit/circuit-json#644.

## Validation

- focused cache/export integration test, including cache hit, source invalidation, and forced explicit rebuild
- `bun test tests/cli/build/build.test.ts tests/cli/check tests/shared` (46 passing)
- snapshot and simulate integration tests (2 passing)
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`

title: Fix decoupling caps wired only to nets being grouped to the wrong chip
description: Fixes incorrect grouping of decoupling capacitors wired only to nets, ensuring they are associated with the correct chip they decouple.
body: Follow-up to #155. That PR added a rail-sharing fallback so a bulk cap wired only to a power rail (no pin-to-pin edge) could still join the chip it decouples — e.g. the RP2040's C9, wired to `net.V1_1` only, joining U3's V1_1 group. The fallback worked for that case but was too broad, and regressed other schematics. ## The bug `findRailSharingMainChipId` picked "the multi-pin chip with the most power pins on the rail." That's not the same as "the chip being decoupled." On any rail, a **connector** qualifies just as well as the MCU: ``` repro125: C1, C2 (wired only to net.VDD / net.GND) -> grouped to J1, a ❌ ``` A pin header isn't decoupled by anything, so this invented a decoupling group (and a cap row) that never existed before. The knock-on layout churn is what showed up a regressions: in repro125 J2 was shoved aside and its GND label stopped routing; in the RP2040 power-supply section C4/C18 were force-rowed and the D1 → regulator → PWR_LED path broke. More fundamentally, none of these caps should have been grouped at all — they're wired net-only with no directly-wired sibling, so before #155 they were placed individually. ## The fix A rail-only cap decouples the **same chip that a directly-wired sibling cap on the same rail decouples**. So instead of scoring candidate main chips, find a sibling capacitor on the rail and ask *it* for its main chip: ```ts private findRailSharingMainChipId(capChip, netPair): ChipId | null { for (const sibling of Object.values(this.inputProblem.chipMap)) { if (sibling.chipId === capChip.chipId) continue if (!sibling.isCapacitor) continue const siblingPair = this.getNormalizedNetPair(sibling) if (!siblingPair || siblingPair[0] !== netPair[0] || siblingPair[1] !== netPair[1]) continue return this.findStronglyConnectedMainChipId(sibling) ?? undefined } return null } ``` - **rp2040**: C9 (V1_1, net-only) finds sibling C18/C7 (also V1_1), which are wired to U3.DVDD → C9 joins U3. ✅ unchanged - **repro125**: C1/C2 (VDD, net-only) have no wired sibling on VDD → left ungrouped → matches the pre-#155 layout. ✅ - **connectors**: the loop only ever inspects capacitors, so a pin header can't be returned as a main chip — it's structurally impossible now, not filtered after the fact. This reuses the existing `findStronglyConnectedMainChipId` and keeps the `findStronglyConnectedMainChipId() ?? findRailSharingMainChipId()` shape, so there's one notion of "which chip does a cap decouple," not two. It's also order-independent — grouping doesn't depend on the order caps are processed (verified with C9 placed first).

title: Use browser-safe PoppyGL rendering for STEP PNG snapshots
description: Updates STEP PNG snapshot rendering to use PoppyGLs browser-safe bitmap and PNG encoder APIs, eliminating the dependency on Node-specific rendering APIs.
body:
## Summary

Updates STEP PNG snapshot rendering to use PoppyGL's browser-safe bitmap and PNG encoder APIs.

## Problem

The snapshot fixture used `pureImageFactory` and `encodePNGToBuffer`, which load Node-specific PureImage and stream dependencies even though the matcher only needs PNG bytes.

## Changes

- Use `createUint8Bitmap` for software-rendered STEP snapshots.
- Use `encodePNG` to produce `Uint8Array` PNG output.
- Preserve the existing snapshot matcher interface and PNG comparison behavior.

## Impact

STEP snapshot generation no longer depends on PoppyGL's Node-only rendering APIs, aligning this test path with the browser-safe 3D PNG rendering pipeline.

## Validation

- `bun test test/basics/basics01/basics01.test.ts`
- Confirmed STEP generation, OCCT validation, rendering, and PNG snapshot matching succeed.

title: Fix browser 3D PNG rendering by removing Node-only PoppyGL exports
description: Fixes browser 3D PNG rendering by removing Node-only rendering APIs from PoppyGLs root entrypoint, allowing for dynamic imports in the browser without Node-specific dependencies.
body:
## Summary

Fixes browser 3D PNG rendering by removing Node-only rendering APIs from PoppyGL's root entrypoint.

## Problem

Importing `poppygl` in the browser included Node-specific buffer-rendering exports and their PureImage/stream dependency chain. This caused browser 3D PNG downloads to fail while dynamically loading PoppyGL through jscdn.

## Changes

- Remove Node-only buffer-rendering exports from the root module.
- Keep the public root API focused on portable `Uint8Array` rendering APIs.
- Route custom-fetch coverage through the universal URL renderer.

Supplying only wasmBinary is insufficient because Emscripten resolves the filename first; the fallback therefore supplies a harmless locateFile result as well. This blocks tscircuiteval3188 and the downstream core  eval  runframe  cli  tscircuit release chain.  Verification npm test npm run test:browser: Chromium bundles the package with esbuild, initializes WASM over HTTP, and verifies a CrossSection area operation npm pack --dry-run --json git diff --check linked this package into the exact dependency update from tscircuiteval3188 bun run build in eval bun run test:playwright -- --grep copper pour successfully renders in eval: 1 passed The package still has no production, optional, or peer dependencies.
body:
## What changed

- embed a generated copy of the vendored Manifold WASM for browser and worker runtimes
- pass both `wasmBinary` and `locateFile` to Emscripten so it never falls back to `new URL("manifold.wasm", import.meta.url)` in bundled/blob workers
- keep the existing filesystem-based Node path and explicit `setWasmUrl()` override
- regenerate and verify the embedded module whenever the vendored Manifold version changes
- add both a browser-like unit regression and a real Playwright browser-bundle test
- add pull-request CI that installs Chromium and runs the unit, type, vendor, and Playwright suites

## Root cause

`@tscircuit/copper-pour-solver@0.0.37` calls the zero-argument `getManifoldModule()` API. In eval's bundled blob worker, Emscripten cannot derive a valid base from `import.meta.url`, so its default WASM lookup throws `TypeError: Failed to construct 'URL': Invalid URL`. Supplying only `wasmBinary` is insufficient because Emscripten resolves the filename first; the fallback therefore supplies a harmless `locateFile` result as well.

This blocks tscircuit/eval#3188 and the downstream core → eval → runframe → cli → tscircuit release chain.

## Verification

- `npm test`
- `npm run test:browser`: Chromium bundles the package with esbuild, initializes WASM over HTTP, and verifies a CrossSection area operation
- `npm pack --dry-run --json`
- `git diff --check`
- linked this package into the exact dependency update from tscircuit/eval#3188
- `bun run build` in eval
- `bun run test:playwright -- --grep "copper pour successfully renders"` in eval: **1 passed**

The package still has no production, optional, or peer dependencies.

title: Fix external schematic traces connected through boxed subcircuit ports
description: Fixes schematic trace generation when an external component connects to a port on a subcircuit rendered with showAsSchematicBox
body: ## Summary Fix schematic trace generation when an external component connects to a port on a subcircuit rendered with `showAsSchematicBox`. ## Problem The schematic trace solver input did not correctly include nested subcircuits represented as schematic boxes. As a result, traces crossing a boxed subcircuit boundary through a port could be missing or routed incorrectly. ## Before and after | Before: missing schematic trace | After: connected through the boxed subcircuit port | | --- | --- | | | | ## Fix - Traverse nested component groups when building schematic trace solver input - Include subcircuits rendered as schematic boxes in the current solver scope - Stop traversal at subcircuit boundaries so their internal descendants remain isolated - Preserve traversal through regular nested groups ## Regression coverage Added a visual schematic regression test connecting an external resistor to a boxed subcircuit port backed by an internal resistor. This reproduces the original failure and verifies the complete external-to-internal schematic connection. ## Validation - Targeted regression test passes - Full test suite passes - TypeScript type checking passes

title: Fix schematic auto-layout text overlap for rotated components with long names
description: Fixes text overlap in schematic auto-layout for rotated components with long names, ensuring proper spacing and readability.
body: In schematic auto-layout, a component whose name/value label is long enough (e.g. a voltage source named VBUS20_ECOK_IN_OUT, or a resistor RESIS1_ECOK_IN_OUT) overlaps the component next to it when auto-layout rotates it — which happens for any 2-pin part with a power/ground pin (the part gets stood upright so its ground pin faces down). Short-named parts (R2, C1) were unaffected. Root cause The text-measurement itself was never wrong — getSchematicComponentWithTextBounds correctly covers a part's label in any orientation (the bounding-box tests prove this at 0/90/180/270°). The bug was in how auto-layout used it: @tscircuit/matchpack reserves each part's footprint while the part is still in its starting orientation, then rotates the whole box to the part's final position. But a symbol draws its label on a different side depending on orientation (a resistor's name sits above it when flat, but to the side when upright). So rotating the "name-above" box puts the reserved space on the wrong axis: RESIS1_ECOK_IN_OUT measured flat : 2.16 wide × 0.68 tall (name reserved horizontally, above) matchpack rotates that box 90° : 0.68 wide × 2.16 tall (reservation now vertical) but the upright name is horizontal, to the side → needs 2.16 wide → overlaps the neighbor The error between "rotated starting box" and "correct final box" scales with label length. For short names it's smaller than the gap matchpack keeps between parts, so it was harmless; for long names it far exceeds the gap, so the name runs into the next part. Fix Make auto-layout measure the label footprint in the part's final (post-rotation) orientation — the same measurement the bounding-box tests use, but with the rotation applied — so the reserved space matches where the label will actually be drawn. - getSchematicComponentWithTextBounds now accepts a ccwRotationDegrees and measures against the rotated symbol variant (which is where the symbol set actually places the label). - Auto-layout determines each part's locked rotation up front (from its ports' nets) and sizes each chip once in its final orientation — a single pass, no re-measure. - The rotated reservation is only applied when it grows past matchpack's inter-chip gap (chipGap). Anything smaller is provably absorbed by that gap and can't overlap a neighbor, so short-labeled parts keep their existing layout and unrelated components don't shift. Why it's needed Without it, any auto-laid-out board with power/ground-connected parts that have descriptive names produces schematics where the names collide with adjacent components — unreadable and misleading. The fix is scoped so it only affects parts whose label genuinely sticks out far enough to overlap; everything else is byte-identical. Before: After: REF:

title: fix schematic box explicit port connections
description: Fixes the issue where explicit schematic box ports did not correctly map to their canonical physical PCB targets, ensuring accurate routing and representation in the PCB layout.
body:
## Summary

- make the parent group `connections` map the canonical target for explicit schematic-box ports
- resolve logical group ports to a deterministic physical PCB target in automatic, sequential, and manual routing
- add a post-layout `PcbPortFinalization` phase so exported group `pcb_port` coordinates and layers match their final physical target, including nested and asynchronously loaded layouts
- include only referenced, same-sheet schematic-box boundary components in the parent schematic solver
- avoid generated connectivity-key labels on otherwise unused box pins

## Root cause

When an explicit `<port>` matched a `connections` key, port synthesis correctly avoided creating a duplicate but the explicit port did not use the group connection as its physical target. Group ports were also rendered before packing completed, while parent groups could move a child subcircuit later. PCB routing consequently saw missing or stale group-port coordinates. The parent schematic solver did not include the referenced boxed boundary component because normal selectors stop at subcircuit boundaries.

## Implementation

This version treats group ports as logical aliases with one canonical physical-target resolver. The same resolver is used for exported PCB ports, trace-by-trace routing, and manual path anchors/waypoints. A dedicated post-layout phase finalizes group PCB ports after all layouts and footprint fetches settle. Schematic scope is expanded only for directly referenced, same-sheet box boundary components rather than traversing arbitrary descendants.

## Adversarial review

A separate agent reviewed the implementation three times. Its findings drove the post-layout lifecycle phase, canonical target precedence, PCB-capable multi-target selection, manual selector coverage, same-sheet schematic scoping, box-specific label suppression, and async phase dependencies. After the final dependency fix, it reported no remaining material blocker.

## Validation

- focused group/port, sequential routing, manual path, and multi-sheet suite: 30 passed
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun run build`
- full suite: 1,154 passed, 31 skipped, 5 resource-sensitive failures; all 5 passed on immediate individual retry
- visual review of six new PCB/schematic snapshots

Stacked on the reproduction-only PR so this PR's diff contains the implementation and regression coverage.

title: Rotate silkscreen pills by their ccw_rotation

body: Silkscreen pills ignored `ccw_rotation` in two places: the renderer drew them axis-aligned, and getComprehensivePcbBounds measured their unrotated box so a rotated pill could extend past the bounds and clip at the viewport. title: Server render package pages
description: Server-render semantic package content into the initial HTML response instead of returning only the SPA loader, ensuring meaningful semantic HTML is returned for every public package page, improving accessibility and SEO.
body:
## Summary

- server-render semantic package content into the initial HTML response instead of returning only the SPA loader
- cover package roots, directory and file routes, releases, release previews, build lists/details, settings, and the legacy package URL
- include route-specific important content such as package metadata, directory listings, README/source text, release metadata, and build status
- preload the same package, release, file, and build records into React Query for the interactive client render
- safely escape rendered content and inline JSON to prevent package data from injecting markup or closing the preload script
- route package paths containing extensions and dotted versions through the local SSR middleware

## Why

Package routes previously injected API data and SEO tags but left the root element empty. Clients without JavaScript—including `curl`, link unfurlers, crawlers, and accessibility tooling—received a loading overlay rather than the actual package content. File URLs such as `blob/index.tsx` and dotted release versions were also mistaken for static assets by the local middleware.

## User and developer impact

Every public package page now returns meaningful semantic HTML on the first response. The browser still transitions into the existing interactive React application, while non-JavaScript consumers can read package metadata and the primary content for the requested route directly from the response body.

## Validation

- `bun test ./bun-tests/package-page-ssr.test.js` (17 tests)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run lint`
- `bun run build`
- verified raw HTML with `curl` for package, directory, `.tsx` file, releases, dotted release version, preview, builds, and build-detail routes
- browser-tested a fresh server-rendered file route through the interactive client render, including the correct release content and hidden loader

title: Add URL-backed package file browsing
description: Add URL-backed package directory routes under :author:packageNametree... and read-only package file routes under :author:packageNameblob... allowing users to navigate package directories and files with shareable URLs, and view files in a dedicated viewer instead of directly opening the editor.

## Summary

- add URL-backed package directory routes under `/:author/:packageName/tree/...`
- add read-only package file routes under `/:author/:packageName/blob/...`
- render text, code, and Markdown files in a dedicated package file viewer instead of opening the editor
- add a top-right **Open in Editor** action for users who want to edit the displayed file
- preserve release selection while navigating and encode special characters in paths safely

## Why

Package directory navigation was stored only in local component state, so directory views could not be linked, refreshed, or represented in browser history. File clicks also jumped directly into the editor even when the user only wanted to inspect a file.

## User impact

Package directories and files now have shareable URLs. Breadcrumbs and parent-directory navigation update the URL, and direct file links show a read-only preview within the package page. Hidden generated directories remain browsable when opened directly, and the file header provides an explicit path into the editor when needed.

## Validation

body:
## Summary

- remove the legacy Winterspec/Fly web application, its route handlers, server middleware, generated OpenAPI file, deployment artifacts, and route-server tests
- retain the root SQLite/derived-table pipeline used by the Cloudflare D1 synchronization workflow
- expose Spring Clamp Terminal Blocks through `cf-proxy` with pitch and pin filters, D1 types, route/response mappings, homepage rendering, and worker tests
- include `spring_clamp_terminal_block` in the D1 synchronization table list
- simplify CI and documentation around the Cloudflare-only architecture

## Why

Production is served exclusively by the Cloudflare worker, but component pages were still being added to the unused legacy server. This removes that duplicate surface and makes the Spring Clamp route part of the production worker implementation.

## Validation

- `bun run format:check`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`

## Summary

title: Fix zero-ohm resistor simulations
description: Fixes the representation of zero-ohm resistors in SPICE netlists by emitting a clamped resistance value of 1e-12  to avoid simulation warnings and ensure correct circuit behavior.
body:
## Summary

- represent zero-ohm resistors as `1e-12 Ω` in generated SPICE netlists
- add an integration regression test that runs a resistor-divider simulation containing a zero-ohm resistor

## Root cause

The converter emitted a literal resistance of `0`. ngspice clamps that value internally to `1e-12 Ω` and prints a warning, while other SPICE implementations may reject an ideal zero-resistance element. Emitting ngspice's clamp value directly preserves short-circuit behavior without the warning.

## User impact

Circuits can retain a normal resistor component with `resistance={0}` for jumpers and optional links while still running SPICE simulations cleanly.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 49 passed
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run build`

title: Size discovered thermal pads explicitly
description: Detects and sizes exposed thermal pads based on their area relative to perimeter pads, improving footprint accuracy for QFNMLP components.
body:
## Summary

- detect a centered exposed pad by its area relative to the perimeter pads
- seed QFN/MLP/quad candidates with explicit `thermalpad{width}x{height}` dimensions
- keep the boolean thermal-pad seed as a shorter candidate when footprinter's default already matches
- add a regression test for a 56-pin QFN with a 3.1 mm square exposed pad

## Root cause

Discovery previously recognized only that a center pad existed and emitted boolean `_thermalpad`. Footprinter then derived the exposed-pad size from the package's inner body dimensions. For C2040 this produced a 5.292 mm pad instead of EasyEDA's 3.10 mm pad, and the optimizer could only compensate by distorting unrelated package dimensions.

## Impact

The discovered C2040 string now contains `thermalpad3.1mmx3.1mm`. Its generated exposed pad is 3.10 × 3.10 mm and the complete footprint reaches 99.708% copper IoU through the deployed high-resolution comparison API, up from approximately 45.6%.

## Validation

- `bun test tests/footprintDiscovery.test.ts` (5 tests)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- live EasyEDA discovery for C2040 with exact 3.10 × 3.10 mm exposed-pad sizing
- deployed preview API comparison for C2040 at 99.708% copper IoU

title: Add automatic footprinter discovery
description: Add a new API endpoint for automatic discovery of ranked footprinter strings based on JLCPCB part numbers, enhancing the footprint selection process for users.
body:
## Summary

- add `POST /api/discover` for ranked footprinter-string discovery from an exact JLCPCB part number
- classify JLC pad topology and seed the search from the live `@tscircuit/footprinter` family/size catalog
- detect geometry-affecting parameters and refine continuous dimensions with finite-difference gradients and Adam
- rank valid candidates using copper IoU, component/package metadata, and pad geometry
- add a UI flow that fills the best result, shows alternatives, and loads the comparison immediately

## Why

Footprinter strings are parameterized, so exhaustive enumeration is not possible. The hybrid search narrows the discrete family space with PCB-domain reasoning, then treats dimensions such as pitch, width, pad width, and pad length as a numerical optimization problem.

Package metadata is included in ranking so geometrically identical but semantically incorrect families do not win. For example, the live C1093 check selects a parameterized resistor footprint instead of an equivalent MELF shape.

## Validation

- `bun test` (4 topology/discovery tests)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- standalone TypeScript check for `api/discover.ts`
- live Express API request for JLCPCB C1093: resistor result, 100% copper IoU
- in-app browser verification of discovery, candidate selection, and comparison rendering

title: enforce source-only schematic trials
description: Make benchmark results derive exclusively from declarative tscircuit TSX by rejecting candidate sources containing schematic lines or net labels, ensuring only authoritative generated artifacts are scored, and updating documentation accordingly.
body:
## What

Make benchmark results derive exclusively from declarative tscircuit TSX:

- reject candidate source containing `<schematicline>` or `<netlabel>`
- reject generated Circuit JSON containing `schematic_line`, including indirect/dynamic JSX
- ignore candidate-provided Circuit JSON, manifests, SVGs, and PNGs
- delete stale candidate build output
- invoke `tsci build` from the benchmark after the agent finishes
- remove API credentials from the build environment
- copy untouched build Circuit JSON and schematic SVG outside the candidate workspace
- rasterize the untouched SVG to PNG in the harness
- score only those authoritative generated artifacts
- record SHA-256 hashes for TSX, Circuit JSON, SVG, and PNG
- update task prompts and documentation with the source-only rules

## Why

The previous live trial post-processed Circuit JSON: it removed generated schematic traces, changed labels, regenerated SVG, and submitted a PNG from that modified JSON. That result could not establish whether tscircuit TSX itself was expressive enough.

This change makes failures useful for language design: without manual schematic lines, explicit net labels, or post-build mutations, a failed reconstruction can expose a limitation in the agent, component primitives, trace layout, or TSX syntax.

## Validation

- `bun run check` — 7 tests passed
- prior row-9 trial is rejected for forbidden `<netlabel>`
- clean component + `<trace>` smoke trial builds successfully
- generated Circuit JSON, SVG, PNG, and provenance hashes are written under `trial/generated/`
- `git diff --check`

title: make real schematic judging reliable
description: Adjusts the GPT-5.6 judge for real schematic pairs by changing reasoning effort to medium, increasing output allowance to 6,000 tokens, and including response status and incomplete details when a verdict has no output text.
body:
## What

Adjust the GPT-5.6 judge for real schematic pairs:

- use medium reasoning instead of high
- increase the output allowance from 1,200 to 6,000 tokens
- include response status and incomplete details when a verdict has no output text

## Why

The synthetic fixtures completed within the old budget, but a real Open Schematics reference compared with a tsci-agent recreation consumed both 1,200 and 4,000 tokens entirely as reasoning and returned no JSON. Medium reasoning with a 6,000-token allowance completed all three repeated judgments.

## Validation

- `bun run check` — 5 tests passed
- real row-9 comparison completed three GPT-5.6 judge calls
- `git diff --check`

title: add reproducible schematic benchmark harness
description: Add an end-to-end harness for running one Open Schematics recreation trial at a time and scoring the result.
body:
## What

Add an end-to-end harness for running one Open Schematics recreation trial at a time and scoring the result.

- fetches a selected row from `bshada/open-schematics`
- separates public task inputs from held-back reference metadata
- creates isolated, labeled trial directories
- accepts a normalized manifest or tscircuit Circuit JSON
- scores component/value fidelity and optional net connectivity
- uses repeated GPT-5.6 vision judgments for schematic-image equivalence
- sends PNG attachments only; SVG and structured circuit data are never given to the judge
- randomizes image order and records raw verdicts, differences, confidence, response IDs, and agreement
- writes a decomposed, machine-readable `score.json`
- adds a synthetic judge-evaluation suite, strict TypeScript checks, tests, and CI

## Judge design

The model receives two PNG images and a rubric asking it to ignore visual styling while checking components, values, labels, junctions, and connectivity. The default score is the fraction of three `sameCircuit` votes.

The model's self-reported continuous similarity is retained only as diagnostic metadata. Evaluation showed it is not calibrated enough to score subtle electrical errors directly.

Sharp remains a dependency only for SVG-to-PNG rasterization in the synthetic evaluation generator; it is no longer used to compare images.

## Empirical validation

Corrected synthetic evaluation using GPT-5.6:

- 6/6 pair classifications correct
- 18/18 individual judgments correct
- 100% equivalent-circuit recall
- 100% changed-circuit recall
- 100% repeat agreement
- robust to randomized image order
- positives: dark theme, blue theme, widened/restyled layout
- negatives: resistor-value change, open capacitor connection, added grounded load

The sample is intentionally small and synthetic; it establishes feasibility, not production-level accuracy. The continuous similarity scores for real errors remained high (0.79–0.90), which is why binary-vote aggregation is used.

## Other validation

- `bun run check`
- 5 unit tests pass
- real Open Schematics row-0 fixture found 65 components and completed an end-to-end smoke trial
- `git diff --check`

title: fix(crystal): accept pin3 and pin4 connections for four-pin variants
description: Fixes the crystal connection schema to accept pin3 and pin4 connections for four-pin crystal variants, resolving type checking and runtime validation issues.
body:
## Summary

Fixes the crystal connection schema so four-pin crystal variants accept all four numbered pins: `pin1`, `pin2`, `pin3`, and `pin4`.

Previously, `pinVariant="four_pin"` created four runtime ports, but the props schema reused the two-terminal `lrPins` definition. As a result, valid `pin3` and `pin4` connections were rejected during type checking and runtime validation.

## Changes

- Add `pin3` and `pin4` to the supported crystal connection labels
- Preserve the existing `left` and `right` aliases for two-pin crystals
- Add a regression test that parses a four-pin crystal with connections on all four numbered pins
- Regenerate the component type documentation

## Example

```tsx
<crystal
  name="X1"
  frequency="16MHz"
  loadCapacitance="20pF"
  pinVariant="four_pin"
  connections={{
    pin1: "U1.XIN",
    pin2: "net.GND",
    pin3: "U1.XOUT",
    pin4: "net.GND",
  }}
/>
```

## Validation

- All 357 tests pass
- TypeScript typecheck passes
- Biome formatting check passes
- Generated documentation is up to date

title: Reject conflicting netlabel connection props
description: Rejects conflicting netlabel props that provide both net and the legacy connection prop, ensuring only valid inputs reach the core system and adding schema tests for validation.
body:
## Summary

- reject `netlabel` props that provide both `net` and the legacy `connection` prop
- keep `net` with `connectsTo` valid for attaching a named net to ports
- keep `connection` parseable when `net` is absent for compatibility
- add schema tests and regenerate the component-types documentation

## Why

`net` and `connection` currently express conflicting ways to identify/connect a net label. Allowing both lets invalid input reach `@tscircuit/core`, where it can create a trace with an unresolved net selector and throw during rendering.

The shared props schema is the correct boundary for this rule. Consumers now receive a Zod issue on the `connection` path with the message `net and connection cannot be provided together`, allowing core to use its normal failed-component/error-placeholder path.

This supports the core regression tracked in [tscircuit/core#2663](https://github.com/tscircuit/core/pull/2663).

## Validation

- `bun test` (356 passed, 0 failed)
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run check-circular-deps`
- all required documentation/type generation scripts
- `git diff --check`

title: fix: hide collapsed group internal connectivity labels
description: Fixes the issue where internal connectivity labels of collapsed schematic groups are exposed, ensuring that only relevant external named nets and visible connections are rendered.
body:
## Summary

- skip raw connectivity-key fallback labels for direct ports on collapsed schematic groups when every connected source endpoint is inside that group
- preserve labels selected from named source nets, assigned display labels, cross-subcircuit connections, and visible component-port labels
- add a positive regression showing an external trace to `net.ROW1` still renders the named `ROW1` label
- update the internal-only snapshot so `ROW1` remains an open box pin

## Root cause

`insertNetLabelsForPortsMissingTrace` treats any port with a connectivity key but no routed schematic trace as needing a label. For a direct port on a `showAsSchematicBox` group, `connectsTo` creates real source connectivity to hidden child ports, but the connectivity island has no user-facing name. The label-selection chain therefore falls through to the generated `connKey`, leaking the implementation identifier outside the collapsed box.

The fix identifies the actual owning collapsed group through the runtime port tree and verifies that every source trace endpoint connected to the public port is contained inside that group before suppressing only the final connectivity-key fallback.

## Impact

Collapsed schematic boxes keep internal unnamed connectivity encapsulated. PCB routing and source connectivity are unchanged. Explicit external named nets and externally visible connections continue to render normally.

## Stack

- Base reproduction: #2674
- This PR contains the fix and targets the reproduction branch.

## Validation

- `bunx biome check ...`
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- targeted group-port and fallback-net-label tests
- full suite: **1168 passed, 31 skipped, 0 failed**

title: Fix schematic matchpack placement for off-center nested groups
description: Fixes incorrect placement of schematic matchpack for nested groups that are off-center, ensuring proper alignment and bounds synchronization of components within the group.
body:
## Summary

- represent nested schematic groups with a content-derived center and size in matchpack
- use the same proxy center for pin offsets and the final group translation
- move schematic primitives with their nested components and keep group bounds synchronized
- add generic off-origin coverage and an RP2040 Zero regression asserting the C6/C1/C2 rail does not intersect U2

## Root cause

Nested groups computed their matchpack proxy size from occupied component/text bounds, but anchored pins and the final translation to the original `schematic_group.center`. When a group’s occupied bounds were off-origin, the contents escaped the rectangle reserved by matchpack and could overlap neighboring groups.

## Testing

title: Fix peak-to-peak voltage source conversion
description: Derives sine-wave peak amplitude from peak-to-peak voltage when voltage is absent, uses peak-to-peak voltage for unipolar square waves, and preserves voltage precedence for backward compatibility.
body:
## Summary

- derive sine-wave peak amplitude from `peak_to_peak_voltage / 2` when `voltage` is absent
- use `peak_to_peak_voltage` as the high level for supported unipolar square waves
- preserve explicit `voltage` precedence for backward compatibility
- add regression coverage for sine, square, and mixed-property inputs

## Root cause

tscircuit core already writes `peak_to_peak_voltage` into `SimulationAcVoltageSource`, and the circuit-json schema includes the field. The SPICE converter only read `voltage`, so a source declared with only `peakToPeakVoltage` became a zero-amplitude waveform such as `SIN(0 0 60 ...)`.

This could silently disable AC-input power supplies, bridge rectifiers, transformer excitation, inverter references, and other analog simulations while still producing a successful simulation.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 52 pass, 0 fail
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run format:check`
- `git diff --check`
