title: Output SVGs for every analog simulation
description: Generate simulation graph and schematic SVG assets for every simulation experiment, using readable, collision-safe experiment-name suffixes when a circuit contains multiple simulations, while preserving existing single-simulation filenames for backward compatibility.
body:
## Summary

- generate simulation graph and schematic SVG assets for every simulation experiment
- use readable, collision-safe experiment-name suffixes when a circuit contains multiple simulations
- preserve `simulation.svg` and `simulation-schematic.svg` for single-simulation circuits
- make `tsci snapshot --simulation-only` create and compare one graph/schematic snapshot pair per simulation
- report every generated simulation filename in build output

## Why

The CLI asset pipeline previously selected only the first `simulation_experiment`, so later experiments had no exported SVG or snapshot.

## Impact

Multi-simulation builds now emit files such as `simulation-root-fast.svg` and `simulation-schematic-root-fast.svg`. Existing single-simulation filenames remain backward compatible. The behavior of `tsci simulate analog` is unchanged by this PR.

## Validation

- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bun test tests/shared/simulation-svg-assets.test.ts tests/cli/build/build-output-flags.test.ts tests/cli/snapshot/snapshot.test.ts tests/cli/simulate/simulate.test.ts` (37 passing)

title: Preserve undersized topology bridges
description: Preserves low-area RectDiff nodes that connect a target or bridge at least two same-layer regions while continuing to prune sub-0.002 mm coordinate artifacts.
body: sample 15 now solves: ## Summary - preserve low-area RectDiff nodes when they connect a target or bridge at least two same-layer regions - continue pruning sub-0.002 mm coordinate artifacts - add a focused regression test for bridge preservation and numerical-sliver removal ## Root cause Dataset 18 sample 15 contains a 0.9865 mm by 0.006 mm RectDiff slice directly between `cmn_881` and the multilayer region `cmn_176`. `NodeDimensionSubdivisionSolver` removed it because its area was below 0.01 mm². Although the slice is narrow, it is a connectivity bridge: removing it makes `source_trace_57__source_net_57_mst2` statically unreachable. Selective rerip cannot repair immutable topology, so it repeatedly rebuilt the other routes until its iteration limit was exhausted. This PR is stacked on #1625 so it uses the selective-rerip starvation fix pinned there. ## Validation - `bun test tests/node-dimension-subdivision-solver.test.ts tests/node-dimension-subdivision-connectivity-bridge.test.ts --timeout 9999999` — 8 passed - `bun run build` — passed - Pipeline 7, dataset 18, effort 1x, sample 15 — completed in 192.8s with 324 vias; relaxed DRC remains failing as in the baseline - Pipeline 7, dataset 18, effort 1x, sample 5 — completed in 21.2s with 169 vias; relaxed DRC passed - Pipeline 7, dataset 18, effort 1x, sample 1 — completed in 22.2s with 195 vias; its two clearance reports exactly match an explicit run of the parent branch

title: Pin selective rerip starvation fix
description: Fixes starvation issue in selective rerip process by pinning tiny-hypergraph to a specific commit, ensuring proper order of route processing.
body: sample 14 now solves: ## Summary - pin `tiny-hypergraph` to commit `15d0b5ac028de1cd233af7bc09953c8b1df87891` - make the selective-rerip starvation fix from tscircuit/tiny-hypergraph#127 available to Pipeline 7 ## Why Dataset-srj18 sample 14 can repeatedly enqueue newly ripped blocker routes ahead of routes that were already pending. The resulting starvation leaves routes unattempted and eventually exhausts `SelectiveReripTinyHyperGraphSolver`'s iteration budget. The pinned tiny-hypergraph commit preserves pending-route order after a selective rerip, placing newly ripped blockers after the existing queue. ## Validation Pipeline 7 dataset-srj18 benchmark on the published PR branch, explicitly at 1x effort: - sample 1: completed in 29.0s with 195 vias; relaxed DRC failed with 2 violations - sample 5: completed in 24.4s with 169 vias; relaxed DRC passed - sample 14: completed in 191.2s with 352 vias; relaxed DRC failed with 318 violations - aggregate completion: 3/3; timeouts: 0/3; relaxed DRC: 1/3 Sample 1 produced the same 195 vias and the same two DRC violations in a local A/B run with the prior tiny-hypergraph dependency, so the pin did not cause that local result. Sample 5 preserved its DRC pass. Sample 14 no longer exhausted the selective-rerip iteration budget, although its existing routing-quality/DRC issue remains. Additional checks: - `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` passed - installed dependency source verified byte-for-byte against commit `15d0b5ac028de1cd233af7bc09953c8b1df87891` ## Dependency - tscircuit/tiny-hypergraph#127

title: Fix packing obstacle generation for pill-shaped SMT pads
description: Fixes packing obstacle generation for components with pill-shaped SMT pads, ensuring they are correctly represented and do not overlap with automatically placed components.
body:
## Summary

Fix PCB packing for components whose footprints use pill-shaped SMT pads.

`extractPadInfos` previously supported rectangular, circular, and polygon SMT pads, but silently omitted `pill` pads. As a result, fixed components containing only pill-shaped pads produced no pad geometry and could disappear from the packing obstacle model. Automatically placed components could then overlap them.

## Changes

- Extract pill-shaped SMT pads using their width, height, and center.
- Preserve their PCB port and network mapping.
- Ensure fixed `relative_to_group_anchor` components with pill-only footprints generate packing obstacles.
- Add focused unit and integration regression coverage.

## Bug reproduction

The regression test models a fixed component whose footprint contains only a pill-shaped SMT pad.

Before this change:

- `extractPadInfos` returned an empty array.
- The fixed component was omitted from packing obstacles.
- Automatic placement could place another component inside its physical area.

After this change:

- The pill pad is represented with the correct dimensions and position.
- The fixed component produces the expected packing obstacle.
- The placer can keep automatically positioned components outside its occupied area.

## Tests

- Verifies pill-pad dimensions, position, port ID, and network mapping.
- Verifies a fixed pill-only component becomes a packing obstacle.
- Full test suite: 108 passed, 10 skipped, 0 failed.
- Typecheck passed.
- Package build and declaration generation passed.
- Formatting passed.

title: Fix RP2040 crystal trace falling back to net labels
description: Fixes routing failure for the RP2040 crystal connection by ensuring both branches are routed instead of falling back to net labels.
body:
## Summary

- adds a solver-level reproduction extracted from `seveibar__rp2040-zero` for the `X1.XTAL2` / `R8.pin2` / `C17.pin1` connection
- permits a trace that touches an endpoint pin to follow that endpoint component's rectangle boundary
- keeps boundary exceptions scoped to the endpoint component; traces crossing component interiors remain collisions
- routes both MSP branches instead of dropping the X1–R8 branch and triggering fallback net labels in core

Core reproduction: https://github.com/tscircuit/core/pull/2672

## Root cause

The generated X1–R8 elbow is the intended horizontal segment. Because it lies on R8's lower obstacle boundary, `findFirstCollision` treated it as entering R8. The rerouter cannot move this aligned/degenerate elbow, so `SchematicTraceLinesSolver` recorded a failed connection pair. Core then correctly preserved connectivity with two fallback labels.

## Verification

- `bun test` — 106 pass, 4 skipped, 0 fail
- `bunx tsc --noEmit`
- `bunx biome check ...` for all changed TypeScript files
- linked the local solver build into core and rendered both the minimal repro and the full `seveibar__rp2040-zero` project: the target fallback label count is 0 and the `X1.3-R8.2` trace is present

title: Prevent selective rerip route starvation
description: Fixes route scheduling to prevent starvation of pending routes during selective rerips in the autorouting process.
body: sample 14 now solves: ## Summary - preserve already-pending route order after a selective rerip - enqueue newly ripped blocker routes after routes that were already waiting - add a focused test for failed, pending, and ripped route ordering ## Root cause Selective rerip previously placed every newly ripped blocker at the front of the queue, immediately after the failed route. Repeated rerips could therefore keep recycling the same blocker group ahead of untouched routes. In dataset-srj18 sample 14, seven pending routes were never attempted before the solver reached its 2,000,000-iteration limit. The new order is the failed route, previously pending routes, then newly ripped blocker routes. This keeps the failed route retry behavior while preventing repeated rerips from starving the existing queue. ## Impact Pipeline 7 dataset-srj18 sample 14 now completes at 1x effort instead of failing in `SelectiveReripTinyHyperGraphSolver`. The change only affects route scheduling after a selective rerip; it does not add a fallback or relax solver acceptance. Sample 14 still has relaxed DRC violations, which are outside the iteration-exhaustion issue addressed here. ## Validation - Pipeline 7 dataset-srj18 sample 14 at 1x effort: completed in 184.0s with 352 vias; relaxed DRC failed with 318 violations - Pipeline 7 dataset-srj18 sample 1 at 1x effort: patched and original dependency A/B runs both completed with 195 vias and the same two relaxed DRC violations - Pipeline 7 dataset-srj18 sample 5 at 1x effort: completed with 169 vias and passed relaxed DRC - `tscircuit-autorouter`: `tsc -p tsconfig.json --noEmit` passed with the patched dependency - queue-order runtime assertion passed in the autorouter dependency environment The standalone `tiny-hypergraph` focused test and typecheck could not run in this clone because its development dependencies were unavailable after the attempted no-save install.

title: Add Monaco-backed workspace Find/Replace with undoable multi-file edits
description: Adds a complete workspace-wide FindReplace workflow to the Monaco editor with CtrlShiftF and CmdShiftF, allowing users to search, navigate, and safely replace text across an entire multi-file circuit project without manually opening each file.
body:
## Summary

Adds a complete workspace-wide Find/Replace workflow to the Monaco editor with `Ctrl+Shift+F` and `Cmd+Shift+F`.

Users can search, navigate, and safely replace text across an entire multi-file circuit project without manually opening each file.

## Major UX improvement

This introduces an essential multi-file editor workflow:

- Search across every non-hidden text file
- Group results by file with exact line previews
- Navigate directly to the matching line and column
- Match case-sensitive text
- Match complete words
- Search using regular expressions
- Replace an individual match
- Replace every match in one file
- Replace every workspace match
- Collapse and expand file result groups
- Navigate results from the keyboard

Monaco's existing `Ctrl+F` search for the current file remains unchanged.

## Monaco-backed implementation

The implementation uses Monaco's model APIs instead of maintaining a separate search engine:

- `ITextModel.findMatches()` supplies validated ranges and capture groups
- `pushEditOperations()` applies replacements through Monaco's undo stack
- Workspace models are searched directly, including files not currently visible
- Invalid regular expressions produce an actionable error
- Results are capped to prevent excessively large result sets

Regex replacements support capture groups and JavaScript-style replacement tokens such as `$1`, `$&`, and `$$`.

## Safe controlled updates

The reference implementation modifies file objects directly during replacement. This implementation avoids that data-integrity risk:

- Input file objects are never mutated
- Monaco models receive undoable edit operations
- Updated content is sent to the host through `onFileContentChange`
- Inactive files are updated without switching the active editor
- Replace controls are disabled in read-only mode

## Public API

Adds the following imperative editor command:

```ts
editorRef.current?.openWorkspaceSearch()
```

This allows tscircuit.com to render search controls outside the editor while using the same workspace-search experience.

## Keyboard shortcuts

- `Ctrl/Cmd+F`: find within the current file using Monaco
- `Ctrl/Cmd+Shift+F`: find and replace across the workspace
- `ArrowUp` / `ArrowDown`: navigate results
- `Enter`: open the selected result
- `Escape`: close workspace search

## Testing

Adds focused regression coverage for:

- Monaco-backed match ranges
- Hidden and binary file exclusion
- Whole-word search configuration
- Invalid regular-expression handling
- Regex capture-group replacement
- Escaped replacement tokens
- Immutable replacement generation

Validation completed:

- 50 tests pass
- TypeScript typecheck passes
- Library build passes
- Cosmos production export passes
- Formatting and diff checks pass

title: Add external Monaco workspace controls and reliable file operations
description: Adds a clean, externally controlled API for embedding the Monaco workspace editor in tscircuit.com and improves file-management reliability.
body:
## Summary

Adds a clean, externally controlled API for embedding the Monaco workspace editor in tscircuit.com and improves file-management reliability.

## What changed

- Expose a typed editor ref API for focusing, formatting, revealing file locations, and controlling sidebar visibility
- Keep product-specific imports, authentication, and save controls outside the editor
- Consolidate content updates into `onFileContentChange(path, content)`
- Replace compatibility-shaped file callbacks with direct create, rename, and delete operations
- Add explicit file-loading state and progress
- Render file-operation errors inline without requiring a toast provider
- Prevent external controls from using disposed Monaco instances
- Remove unsupported folder rename and delete actions
- Prevent duplicate file-selection callbacks
- Correct runtime and Cosmos fixture dependency ownership

## Bug fix and regression coverage

Reproduces a stale-error bug where a rename first fails because the destination exists, then succeeds with a valid name, but leaves the previous failure visible. Successful rename and delete operations now clear stale errors, with a regression test covering the fail-then-success rename sequence.

## User impact

The editor can now be integrated with external tscircuit.com controls without exposing Monaco internals. File operations provide consistent inline feedback, navigation fires once, and editor commands remain safe across streaming, binary-file, and unmounted states.

## Validation

- `bun test` — 40 tests passing
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run cosmos`
- `bun run cosmos:export`
- Formatting and unused-code checks

title: Audit top-stock JLC footprint coverage
description: Add a live benchmark for top-stock parts across JLC categories, fix two-sided pitch inference, promote certain components to passing regressions, and document coverage snapshots and proposed footprinter strings.
body:
## Summary

- add a live `bun run audit:jlc` benchmark for top-stock parts across seven representative JLC categories
- fix two-sided pitch inference by separating lead pitch from row-to-row span
- promote the C9002 crystal and C139797 switch reproductions to passing regressions
- document the current coverage snapshot and exact proposed footprinter strings for the remaining DSL gaps

## Why

The global JLC stock ranking is dominated by repeated 0402 and 0603 passives, so this audit samples the current leader in representative component categories. The audit converts live EasyEDA footprints to Circuit JSON and fails when copper IoU is below 98%.

The search previously took the median of every repeated coordinate difference. For two-sided and 2-by-2 packages, that blended the actual lead pitch with the much larger distance between rows. Using the lower quartile recovers the regular pitch and produces exact parameterized geometry.

## Current results

- exact: current top-stock switch (C318884), C9002 crystal, and C139797 tactile switch
- existing exact global non-passive two-pad coverage: C2286, C84256, C7420372, C965799
- proposed DSL additions: SOT row span and rotations, global rotations, LQFP/SOIC pill pads, centered 4/3 LGA sides, USON exposed-pad rows, and a USB-C receptacle family

## Validation

- `bun run format:check`
- `bun run typecheck`
- `bun test`
- `bun run build`
- `bun run audit:jlc` (expected non-zero: 6/7 current category leaders are documented coverage gaps)

## Summary

- assert the desired copper IoU of at least 0.99 as expected-failing tests
- share only the Circuit JSON render/conversion helper and candidate IoU logging

## Reproductions

- C2765186 — TYPE_C_16PIN_2MD_073_ USB-C connector
- C2843335 — W25Q16JVUXIQ flash
- C23380830 — AP2112K_3_3TRG1 regulator
- C9002 — X322512MSB4SI crystal
- C139797 — SKRPACE010 switch

Each part has an independent `.test.tsx` file containing its TSX fixture, rendered-pad-count check, and expected-failing recovery assertion. The `test.failing` cases keep CI green while preserving the desired behavior as executable assertions. When a matcher gap is fixed, Bun reports the unexpected pass so that case can become a normal regression test.

- `bun run format:check`
